coredata.dc:title: Fluorescence quenching mechanism of 9-hydroxyphenal-1-one carbon quantum dots by Cu2+ ions: An experimental and computational investigation
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry
coredata.dc:creator: Zhao, Zhengyan
Zhang, Xiaolu
Song, Xuedan
Hao, Ce
coredata.dc:description: A combination of experimental and computational methods was used to study the Cu2+ ions recognition by 9-hydroxyphenal-1-one carbon quantum dots (HPHN-CQDs). HPHN-CQDs were proved to be an efficient fluorescent probe that can detect Cu2+ ions with high selectivity and sensitivity, and the detection limit was 4.8 nM. We performed a detailed analysis of the fluorescence quenching mechanism of HPHN-CQDs by means of density functional theory (DFT) and time-dependent density functional theory (TDDFT). The orbital interaction diagram indicated that the presence of Cu2+ can affect the luminescence behavior of HPHN by changing its orbital distribution. In addition, the electron was transferred from the excited HPHN to the 3d orbital of Cu2+, which would hinder the recombination of electron-hole pairs and promote the fluorescence quenching of HPHN-CQDs. The photophysical process revealed that the fluorescence rate constant of HPHN-Cu2+ was substantially decreased compared with that of HPHN, which is consistent with the experimental results.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fluorescence sensor
DFT calculation
Quenching mechanism
Photophysical process
Internal charge transfer

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detection of Cu2+ ions using HPHN-CQDs as the fluorescence probe
Cu2+ ions detection based on HPHN-CQDs
The coordination interaction between HPHN and Cu2+
The rate constants in the photophysical process

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) The molecular structure and (b) the crystal structure of HPHN (c) The cluster model of HPHN-CQDs built by Materials studio 8.0 software. Gray, red and white (pink) balls represent C, O and H atoms, respectively.
(a) TEM image of HPHN-CQDs (b) the particle size distribution of HPHN-CQDs by DLS (c) the zeta potential of HPHN-CQDs (d) UV–vis absorption spectra of HPHN-CQDs in water and HPHN in ethanol solution.

(a) The excitation and fluorescence spectra of HPHN-CQDs. The right is the emission image of HPHN-CQDs dispersed in water under a 365 nm UV lamp (b) The fluorescence intensities of HPHN-CQDs for various metal ions. (c) Fluorescence spectra of HPHN-CQDs in different concentrations of Cu2+ ions (d) The Stern–Volmer plot for HPHN-CQDs by gradual addition of Cu2+ ions in water. The inset shows the quenching linearity relationship at low concentrations of Cu2+ ions (0 − 150 nM).
(a) TDOS of HPHN-Cu2+ (b) PDOS of Cu atom (c) PDOS of O atoms. The vertical line corresponds to the HOMO level.

(a) The most stable configuration of HPHN-Cu2+ (b) The orbital interaction diagrams of β spin for HPHN-Cu2+. The numbers next to the red lines indicate the contributions of the two fragments orbital (HPHN and Cu2+) to the HPHN-Cu2+ orbital.
The diagram of photophysical process and the internal charge transfer (ICT) process of HPHN-Cu2+. S0: ground state; S1: excited singlet state.
Comparison of CQDs based fluorescence sensors for Cu2+ ions detection.
The fluorescence emission rate constant (k F), internal conversion rate constant (k IC) and the fluorescence quantum yields (η F) of HPHN and HPHN-Cu2+.

head.ce:title: Fluorescence quenching mechanism of 9-hydroxyphenal-1-one carbon quantum dots by Cu2+ ions: An experimental and computational investigation

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Fine Chemicals, School of Chemical Engineering, Dalian University of Technology, Dalian 116024, Liaoning, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Fine Chemicals

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Fine Chemicals, School of Chemical Engineering, Dalian University of Technology, Dalian 116024, Liaoning, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The fluorescence quenching mechanism of the HPHN-CQDs in the presence of Cu2+ ions.

A combination of experimental and computational methods was used to study the Cu2+ ions recognition by 9-hydroxyphenal-1-one carbon quantum dots (HPHN-CQDs). HPHN-CQDs were proved to be an efficient fluorescent probe that can detect Cu2+ ions with high selectivity and sensitivity, and the detection limit was 4.8 nM. We performed a detailed analysis of the fluorescence quenching mechanism of HPHN-CQDs by means of density functional theory (DFT) and time-dependent density functional theory (TDDFT). The orbital interaction diagram indicated that the presence of Cu2+ can affect the luminescence behavior of HPHN by changing its orbital distribution. In addition, the electron was transferred from the excited HPHN to the 3d orbital of Cu2+, which would hinder the recombination of electron-hole pairs and promote the fluorescence quenching of HPHN-CQDs. The photophysical process revealed that the fluorescence rate constant of HPHN-Cu2+ was substantially decreased compared with that of HPHN, which is consistent with the experimental results.

ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescence probes, such as carbon quantum dots (CQDs) sensor [1,2] and organic sensor [3,4], have been developed for the detection of metal ions in recent years. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) [5,6] are particularly obtained the attention by researchers due to their low toxicity, environmental friendliness, and highly stable fluorescence [7,8]. The CQDs that have been reported as fluorescence probes for the detection of metal ions are usually doped with metal complexes or decorated with additional functional groups [9–13]. The fluorescence of CQDs is usually quenched by these metal ions [14–16], and the quenching mechanism is mainly attributed to the formation of nonfluorescent complexes. However, most of the reported literatures focus mainly on experiments, and little attention has been given to calculations of the quenching mechanism. In addition, CQDs that have not been modified with other groups and have known structures are rarely reported as fluorescent probes for the detection of metal ions, which are quite suitable for the calculation of quenching mechanism. Research on the mechanism of fluorescence quenching is crucial.

Copper, as an important transition metal and an essential trace element for the human body, plays a vital role in various biological processes [17,18]. However, excessive Cu2+ ions may cause potential risks to human health and the environment [19,20]. The United States Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) has set the maximum acceptable level of Cu2+ ions in drinking water is 20 μM [21]. Therefore, the development of facile, efficient and green methods for Cu2+ ions detection is necessary.

Herein, we used a simple method to convert a 9-hydroxyphenal-1-one compound with known molecular and crystal structures into HPHN-CQDs. The HPHN-CQDs showed excellent selectivity and high sensitivity to Cu2+ ions, and Cu2+ ions strongly quenched the fluorescence of HPHN-CQDs compared with other metal ions. DFT and TDDFT methods, such as the orbital interaction diagram analysis of HPHN-Cu2+ and the internal charge transfer (ICT) between HPHN and Cu2+ in the excited state were utilized to investigate the mechanism of the fluorescence quenching. In addition, the rate constants of fluorescence and internal conversion in the photophysical processes of HPHN and HPHN-Cu2+ were also calculated. The combined experimental and theoretical approach utilized herein can provide comprehensive insight into the quenching mechanism of HPHN-CQDs by Cu2+ ions.

HPHN was obtained from the international laboratory (IL, USA) as a yellow powder with a purity of 97 %. NaCl, KCl, CaCl2, MgCl2·6H2O, FeCl3·6H2O, NiCl2·6H2O, CoCl2·6H2O, AgCl, CrCl3·6H2O, ZnCl2, CuCl2·2H2O, and MnCl2·4H2O were obtained from Kermel Co., Ltd. and were analytical grade. All chemicals were used directly without further purification.

The preparation method of HPHN-CQDs has been reported in our previous work [22]. Briefly, 1 mg HPHN was dispersed in 300 mL deionized water and sonicated with a common ultrasonic instrument at 300 W for 2 h, followed by ultrasonic dispersion at 400 W for 10 min to obtain a homogeneous suspension. Then, yellow HPHN-CQDs were obtained after centrifugation.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM, FEI Tecnai F30) was used to obtain the TEM images at 300 kV. The size distribution of particles and the zeta potential were measured by dynamic light scattering (Zetasizer Nano ZS, Malvern Instruments Ltd., USA). The measurement range of the particle size is 0.6 − 6000 nm. Lambda750S (Perkin Elmer, USA) and Pro 97 (Shanghai, China) were used to measure the UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectra. The fluorescence quantum yield was measured by using a Quantaurus-QY Plus UV-NIR absolute quantum yield spectrometer (Hamamatsu C11347).

In a typical experiment, different metal ions (Na+, K+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Fe3+, Ni2+, Co2+, Ag+, Cr3+, Zn2+, Mn2+) were mixed with 2 mL HPHN-CQDs, respectively. The final concentrations of various metal ions in HPHN-CQDs were 0.8 μM. After the mixtures were stabilized at 25 °C for 10 min, the fluorescence spectra under excitation at 356 nm were recorded.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Cu2+ ions using HPHN-CQDs as the fluorescence probe
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescent probe HPHN-CQDs (2 mL) were mixed with different concentrations of Cu2+ ions (0 − 500 nM). The fluorescence spectra were recorded by a fluorescence spectrophotometer.

Gaussian 09 software [23] was used to calculate the geometry optimization and the frequency using the Becke-type three-parameter Lee-Yang-Parr (B3LYP) [24] hybrid functional with the 6−31 G (d,p) [25] and Lanl2dz [26,27] basis sets for C, H, O atoms and metal atoms, respectively. The continuum SMD implicit solvation model [28] was employed to simulate the water medium, and the default parameters are provided in the Supporting Information (Computational details). The geometries of S0 and S1 states were calculated using DFT and TDDFT, respectively. The charges set before and after the presence of the Cu atom were 0.0 and 2.0, respectively. The corresponding spin multiplicities were 1 and 2, respectively.

The Molecular Materials Property Prediction Package (MOMAP) program [29,30] was used to simulate the absorption and fluorescence spectra, and calculate the fluorescence and internal conversion rate constants. The calculation details are given in the Supporting Information (Computational details). The density of states, charge decomposition analysis and orbital interaction diagram analysis were all calculated using the Multiwfn program [31] (version 3.3.4).

The molecular structure of HPHN is shown in Fig. 1 a and was obtained from its crystal structure [32], shown in Fig. 1b. HPHN is a tricyclic structure consisting of carbon, oxygen and hydrogen atoms, including carbonyl and hydroxyl functional groups.

The HPHN-CQDs prepared by the ultrasonic method were characterized by TEM in Fig. 2 a and Fig. S1, and the diameter of the particle size for HPHN-CQDs was approximately 10 nm. Furthermore, the particle size of the dynamic light scattering (DLS) measurement for HPHN-CQDs in Fig. 2b was approximately 21 nm. The particle size measured by DLS was slightly larger than that measured by TEM, which might be attributed to the size measured in DLS technique is the hydrodynamic diameter of the theoretical sphere [33–35]. We also established the HPHN-CQDs cluster model in Fig. 1c based on the size of HPHN-CQDs, and this model contains approximately ten thousand HPHN molecules. The zeta potential of HPHN-CQDs in Fig. 2c was −48.6 mV, indicating the HPHN-CQDs we prepared are stability. Fig. 2d shows the UV–vis spectra of HPHN-CQDs and HPHN solution both have absorption in the UV and visible regions. For the CQDs, the absorption features shift to lower energy with the increasing of the particle size [36,37]. In addition, because of the small size of the clusters, the geometric configuration and interatomic forces on the surface of the clusters are completely different from those in the bulk phase. This may the reason that cause the red shift of UV–vis absorption spectra for HPHN-CQDs dispersed in water than that of HPHN in ethanol solution.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cu2+ ions detection based on HPHN-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence spectra of HPHN-CQDs at different excitation wavelengths from 320 nm to 380 nm are shown in Fig. S2. The maximum fluorescence emission peak of HPHN-CQDs was observed at 451 nm with an excitation wavelength of 356 nm (Fig. 3 a), corresponding to a strong blue fluorescence under UV excitation. The fluorescence quantum yield of HPHN-CQDs is measured to be 1.1 %. The selectivity of HPHN-CQDs as fluorescence probes for variousmetal ions (Na+, K+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Fe3+, Ni2+, Co2+, Ag+, Cr3+, Zn2+, Mn2+) was studied, as shown in Fig. 3b (F 0 and F are the fluorescence intensities in the absence and presence of metal ions, respectively). The final concentration of the various metal ions in HPHN-CQDs was 0.8 μM. It is clear that the fluorescence intensity of most metal ions did not obviously change. In contrast, the fluorescence intensity of HPHN-CQDs was quenched by nearly 97 % in the presence of Cu2+ ions, indicating the higher selectivity of HPHN-CQDs toward Cu2+ ions than other metal ions. The above results demonstrate the outstanding selectivity of HPHN-CQDs to Cu2+ ions.

Sensitivity is another important parameter for the fluorescence probe HPHN-CQDs detection of Cu2+ ions. The fluorescence responses of the HPHN-CQDs upon addition of different concentrations of Cu2+ ions were investigated, as illustrated in Fig. 3c. The fluorescence intensity of the HPHN-CQDs gradually decreased with the increasing concentration of Cu2+ ions from 0 to 500 nM, indicating that Cu2+ ions can effectively quench the fluorescence of HPHN-CQDs. The well-known Stern–Volmer equation, F0 /F = 1 + K SV[Cu2+], can be used to evaluate the quenching effect of HPHN-CQDs by Cu2+, as shown in Fig. 3d (F 0 and F are the fluorescence intensities before and after the addition of Cu2+ ions, respectively, K SV is the Stern-Volmer quenching constant, and [Cu2+] is the concentration of Cu2+ ions (nM)). The SV plot displays nearly linear at low concentrations of Cu2+ ions, however, at higher concentrations, the curve becomes nonlinear due to the energy-transfer process or self-absorption [38,39]. The linear relationship was observed between (F 0 – F)/F and the concentration of Cu2+ ions at low concentration ranges (0 nM–150 nM), and the K SV is 0.0095. The limit of detection (LOD) of Cu2+ ions was obtained using the formula 3δ/S (δ is the standard deviation of the blank experiment and S is the slope of the calibration plot). The calculated detection limit of Cu2+ ions was 4.8 nM, which is lower than most reported Cu2+ ions detection results (Table 1 ). Therefore, HPHN-CQDs are promising fluorescent probes that can detect Cu2+ ions with high selectivity and high sensitivity.

The fluorescence quenching of carbon quantum dots is usually caused by the aggregation of the metal ions. However, in our case, the results of TEM and DLS showed that there was almost no change in the particle size of HPHN-CQDs before and after the addition of Cu2+ ions, as shown in Fig. S3; thus, this mechanism was ruled out.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The coordination interaction between HPHN and Cu2+
ce:section.ce:para: The HPHN molecule has a known crystal structure. There are certainly implementations of, e.g. Quantum ESPRESSO package [45] combined with YAMBO code [46], ABINIT software [47], Exciting code [48] or GPAW program [49], TDDFT for periodic-boundary-condition codes available. However, many programs are still unable to calculate the photophysical process. In addition, Fig. 1c shows that HPHN-CQDs are a large cluster, which is difficult to calculate its properties in the excited state. Therefore, a single HPHN molecule was used as the motif in our calculation. As shown in Fig. S4, the calculated UV–vis absorption, fluorescence and IR spectra of the motif match well with the experimental values, indicating that the calculation model we used is reasonable. For the complex formed by HPHN and Cu2+ ions (HPHN-Cu2+), several different coordination modes were calculated, and finally we acquired three different structures (Table S2). The most stable structure of HPHN-Cu2+ is selected and given in Fig. 5a, in which two Cu-O bonds are formed between HPHN and Cu2+.

To understand the coordination interaction between HPHN and Cu2+, we analyzed the DOS of HPHN-Cu2+, as shown in Fig. 4
. The DOS of the α spin and β spin of HPHN-Cu2+ are almost the same. Fig. 4b and Fig. 4c show that the PDOS spectra of Cu and O atoms overlap, mainly because the 3d orbital of the Cu atom interacts with the 2p orbitals of the O atoms, resulting in the formation of coordination bonds between the Cu atom and O atoms. In addition, the calculated IR spectra for HPHN-Cu2+ provided in Fig. S5a shows two characteristic peaks at 472 cm−1 and 678 cm−1, which were attributed to the stretching vibrations of Cu-O bonds. The above results demonstrated the formation of coordination bonds between HPHN and Cu2+.

Orbital interaction diagram analysis is an effective approach to further clarify the coordination behavior of HPHN-CQDs with Cu2+. The orbital interaction diagram of the α spin for HPHN-Cu2+ is shown in Fig. S6; because its change was very small, we mainly study the orbital interaction diagram of the β spin, as is presented in Fig. 5b. Fig. 5b shows the contributions of two fragments (HPHN and Cu2+) to the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) and the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) of HPHN-Cu2+ complex. The β−HOMO of HPHN-Cu2+ is mainly concentrated on the HPHN fragment (96 %), and there is almost no distribution on the Cu2+ fragment. In contrast, the β-LUMO is mainly located on Cu2+ fragment, and a small portion is located on HPHN fragment (76 % Cu2+ fragment and 19 % HPHN fragment), which is different from the LUMO distribution of HPHN. The presence of Cu2+ can affect the LUMO distribution of HPHN, and therefore affect the luminescence behavior of HPHN.

Taking the divalent metal ions Zn2+, Ca2+ and Mg2+ as examples, we optimized their structures after coordination with HPHN molecules, denoted as HPHN-Zn2+, HPHN-Ca2+ and HPHN-Mg2+, respectively. Their corresponding orbital interaction diagrams are shown in Fig. S7. Compared with HPHN, the HOMO and LUMO distributions for HPHN-Zn2+, HPHN-Ca2+ and HPHN-Mg2+ are almost unchanged, which is different from Cu2+ and may not affect the luminescence behavior of HPHN. This difference may be related to the coordination behavior between metal ions and HPHN.

In addition, compared with the gap of HPHN, the gap between β-LUMO and β−HOMO of HPHN-Cu2+ is clearly decreased, while for other metal ions, there is little change. According to the research of Zhao et al. [50], it is beneficial for the transition of the molecules from the S1 to the S0 states in the form of internal conversion, and is not beneficial for luminescence, which would cause the fluorescence quenching phenomenon.

Furthermore, we optimized the structure of HPHN-Cu2+ in the S1 state and utilized the extended charge decomposition analysis (ECDA) method proposed by Gorelsky et al. [51] to calculate the electron transfer between HPHN and Cu2+. The calculation results show that the electron is transferred from HPHN to Cu2+ and the net amount of electron transferred from HPHN to Cu2+ was 0.48, the schematic diagram of the internal charge transfer (ICT) process is shown in Fig. 6 . The electron in the LUMO of the excited S1 state of HPHN will be transferred to the LUMO of the Cu2+, mainly to the half-filled 3d orbitals of the Cu2+. The energies of the HOMO, LUMO and the energy gaps of HPHN and Cu2+ are listed in Table S3. The electron transfer from HPHN to Cu2+ would hinder the recombination of electron-hole pairs in HPHN, resulting in fluorescence quenching phenomenon.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The rate constants in the photophysical process
ce:section.ce:para: The combination of HPHN-CQDs and Cu2+ ions is a spontaneous process in the ground state (ΔG = −11.295 kcal/mol < 0 and the calculation process of this reaction in the ground state is shown Table S1 in the Computational details in SI), indicating this process is not a photochemical reaction. The decrease in the fluorescence intensity of HPHN-CQDs by Cu2+ ions can be caused by a series of processes. We studied the photophysical processes of HPHN and HPHN-Cu2+, which is dominated by the lowest-lying excited state according to Kasha's rule [52]. The diagram of the photophysical process in Fig. 6 shows that the molecular is first excited from the S0 to the S1 states, and there are two different transition modes return from the S1 to the S0 states, mainly radiative decay and nonradiative internal conversion (IC).

We calculated the rate constants of fluorescence emission (k F) and internal conversion (k IC) for HPHN and HPHN-Cu2+, as shown in Table 2 . It can be seen that the k F for HPHN and HPHN-Cu2+ are 8.72 × 107 s−1 and 2.63 × 10 s−1, and the k IC for HPHN and HPHN-Cu2+ are 4.48 × 108 s−1and 3.17 × 1013 s−1, respectively. After the addition of Cu2+, the k F of HPHN decreased by approximately six orders of magnitude, while k IC increased by approximately five orders of magnitude. The degree of decrease in k F is almost the same as the degree of increased in k IC, which indicates that the competition in the S1 state was mainly between these two processes. It is clear that the fluorescence intensity of HPHN-Cu2+ deceased dramatically compared with that of HPHN, HPHN-Cu2+ mainly returned from the S1 to the S0 states by internal conversion. Among them, the calculated η F (fluorescence quantum yield) of HPHN is 16.3 %, the absolute fluorescence quantum yield is measured in the experiment and the value of η F for HPHN is 1.1 %, which is an order of magnitude lower than that of the calculated value. There is still a certain deviation between the experiment and calculation values. The calculated η F of HPHN-Cu2+ is 8.3 × 10−11%, which is close to zero, the η F of HPHN-Cu2+ is also too low to be observed in the experiment.

We also calculated the fluorescence spectra of HPHN-Cu2+ in Fig. S5b, and the fluorescence intensity of HPHN-Cu2+ was almost zero compared with the fluorescence intensity of HPHN. The above calculation results show that the fluorescence intensity of HPHN is greatly weakened after interacting with Cu2+, which is consistent with the experimental results.

We also considered the explicit solvent SMD model to simulate the H2O solvent, and optimized the complexes formed by several water molecules (from one to four) and HPHN-Cu2+. Fig. S8 shows the optimized structures of HPHN-Cu2+ coordinated with one molecule of H2O, two molecules of H2O, three molecules of H2O and four molecules of H2O, denoted as HPHN-Cu2+-H2O, HPHN-Cu2+-2H2O, HPHN-Cu2+-3H2O and HPHN-Cu2+-4H2O, respectively. We calculated the k F and k IC of these four complexes and the results are listed in Table S4. It can be seen that the k F and k IC values of these four complexes were almost the same as those of HPHN-Cu2+, demonstrating that the explicit solvent model has no significant effect on the calculation results of the k F and k IC values. It can be concluded that the implicit solvent model we used is reasonable.

In summary, the HPHN-CQDs we prepared using pure compounds have proven to be an efficient fluorescent probe that can detect Cu2+ ions with high selectivity and sensitivity. HPHN-CQDs with known molecular and crystal structures are suitable for studying its quenching mechanism through quantum chemical calculation methods. DFT and TDDFT calculations revealed that the sensitive detection of Cu2+ ions can be attributed to the coordination bond between HPHN and Cu2+ by the orbital interaction diagram compared with other metal ions. The decrease of the gap of HPHN-Cu2+ compared with that of HPHN is beneficial for the molecules transition from the S1 to the S0 states in the form of internal conversion, and is not beneficial for luminescence. In addition, the electron transfers from the excited HPHN to Cu2+ would hinder the recombination of electron-hole pairs and promote the fluorescence quenching of HPHN. Furthermore, the photophysical process revealed that the fluorescence rate constant of HPHN-Cu2+ was substantially decreased compared with that of HPHN, which is constituent with the experimental results.

Zhengyan Zhao: Conceptualization, Methodology, Software, Data curation, Writing - original draft. Xiaolu Zhang: Visualization, Investigation. Xuedan Song: Supervision. Ce Hao: Software, Validation, Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant Nos. 21677029, 21606040).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jphotochem.2020.113103.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon dots as rapid assays for detection of mercury(II) ions based on turn-off mode and breast milk

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Pajewska-Szmyt, B. Buszewski, R. Gadzała-Kopciuch, Carbon dots as rapid assays for detection of mercury(II) ions based on turn-off mode and breast milk, Spectrochimi. Acta A 236 (2020) 118320.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Efficiency enhancement of bifacial dye-sensitized solar cells through bi-tandem carbon quantum dots tailored transparent counter electrodes

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: JJ.L. Duan, Y.Y. Zhao, B.L. He, Q.W. Tang, Efficiency enhancement of bifacial dye-sensitized solar cells through bi-tandem carbon quantum dots tailored transparent counter electrodes, Electrochim. Acta 278 (2018) 204–209.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Quantum dots derived from two-dimensional materials and their applications for catalysis and energy

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Terbium (III) modified fluorescent carbon dots for highly selective and sensitive ratiometry of stringent

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) modified ZnO/CdS nanoparticles based fluorescence sensor for highly selective and sensitive detection of Fe (III)
